title: Word Senses in Superposition
subtitle: How Arora et al. proved that polysemous word embeddings are weighted sums of sense vectors — and how sparse coding recovers them

children.children: What does the word 'bank' mean? It depends on context — a financial institution, a river's edge, a pool shot, a blood bank. Word2Vec and GloVe assign ONE vector to 'bank.' But which bank is that vector representing?

This paper (Arora, Li, Liang, Ma, Risteski — TACL 2018) gives a stunning answer: the vector represents ALL senses simultaneously. The embedding of 'bank' is literally a weighted average of separate sense vectors, where the weights reflect how frequently each sense appears in the training corpus.
Imagine recording audio at a cocktail party. Your single microphone captures a MIX of all conversations. Each conversation is distinct, but the recording is their superposition (sum).
A polysemous word embedding is like that recording. Multiple 'conversations' (senses) are mixed into one signal (vector). The question is: can you separate them back out?
Arora et al. show that you can — using sparse coding, the mathematical analog of blind source separation.

props.question: Before this paper, what was the standard approach to polysemy in word embeddings?
options: Train separate embeddings for each word sense
Ignore it — one vector per word, regardless of senses
Manually label word senses in the training data
props.explanation: Standard word2vec/GloVe models assign exactly one vector per word token, regardless of how many senses it has. The polysemy is just… absorbed into the vector. Arora et al. showed this absorption has a precise mathematical structure.

children.children: The paper's central claim is elegant:
A polysemous word's embedding is a linear superposition (weighted sum) of its sense vectors, where weights are proportional to sense frequency.
Mathematically: v_bank ≈ f₁·v_financial + f₂·v_river + f₃·v_pool + ... where fᵢ is how often sense i appears in the corpus.
Suppose 'bank' appears 10,000 times in the training corpus:
7,000 times as financial institution (f₁ = 0.70)
2,500 times as river bank (f₂ = 0.25)
500 times as other senses (f₃ = 0.05)
Then: v_bank ≈ 0.70·v_financial + 0.25·v_river + 0.05·v_other
The financial sense dominates because it's most frequent. This is why v_bank is closer to 'money' than to 'shore' in cosine similarity — the financial component has the biggest weight.

props.title: Why Superposition? The Random Walk Model

children.children: The theoretical foundation comes from Arora et al.'s earlier work (2016) on a 'random walk on discourses' model of text generation. The key assumptions:
Text is generated by a 'context vector' c(t) that performs a random walk through embedding space
At each step, the probability of emitting word w is proportional to exp(⟨c(t), v_w⟩) — words aligned with the current context are more likely
Different word senses correspond to different regions of the context space

Under this model, the word embedding v_w ends up being the weighted average of the context vectors where w typically appears. For a polysemous word, this means averaging across MULTIPLE context regions — one per sense. The weights are the relative frequencies of each sense.
This isn't just a heuristic — it's a provable consequence of the generative model. The superposition structure is built into how word embeddings are learned.

props.title: Discourse Atoms: The Hidden Vocabulary of Context

children.children: One of the paper's most novel contributions is the concept of 'discourse atoms' — approximately 2,000 vectors that span the space of possible discourse contexts.
Think of discourse atoms as a vocabulary of CONTEXTS rather than words. Each discourse atom describes a type of discussion or topic. Every word sense is associated with one or a few discourse atoms.
The financial sense of 'bank' is associated with a discourse atom whose top co-occurring words include: money, deposit, loan, account, interest, savings, credit, financial
The river sense is associated with a different discourse atom: water, river, shore, fish, stream, flow, bridge, erosion
Each discourse atom gives you a succinct, human-readable description of what that sense MEANS — not in terms of a dictionary definition, but in terms of what words surround it in practice.

The ~2,000 discourse atoms are found through a clustering/factorization of the co-occurrence matrix. They form an overcomplete basis for the 'discourse space' — the space of possible conversational contexts.
Crucially, word senses are SPARSE in this basis. The financial sense of 'bank' strongly activates maybe 2-3 discourse atoms out of 2,000. This sparsity is what makes recovery possible.

The discourse atoms are conceptually identical to dictionary atoms in sparse coding, or to features in an SAE. This is the first bridge between this paper and the SAE revolution that would come years later.

Discourse atoms are like radio stations. Each station broadcasts a specific type of content (sports, news, classical music, talk radio). A word sense is like a device that picks up 1-2 stations strongly — the financial sense of 'bank' is tuned to the 'finance' and 'commerce' stations.
The polysemous word embedding is like hearing ALL stations at once (weighted by frequency). Sparse coding is the tuner that separates them back out.
Given the superposition structure, the recovery algorithm is surprisingly straightforward:
Start with the polysemous word embedding v_w (768-dim vector)
Use sparse coding to decompose v_w into the discourse atom basis: v_w ≈ Σᵢ αᵢ·aᵢ where aᵢ are discourse atoms and αᵢ are sparse coefficients
Group the active discourse atoms into clusters — each cluster corresponds to a word sense
The sense vector for each cluster is the weighted sum of its discourse atoms
Imagine decomposing 'bank':
Step 2: Cluster the active atoms:
Cluster A (atoms 3, 5): finance-related → v_bank_financial
Cluster B (atoms 847, 912): water/nature-related → v_bank_river
Step 3: Each cluster's weighted sum becomes a sense vector. These sense vectors are MORE accurate for sense-specific similarity tasks than the original mixed embedding.

The mathematical magic: in high-dimensional spaces (768-dim), you can pack many more than 768 nearly-orthogonal directions. With ~2,000 discourse atoms in 768-dim space, most pairs have very low cosine similarity.

Since each sense activates only a FEW atoms (sparsity), and the atoms are nearly orthogonal, the decomposition is essentially unique. This is the same principle that makes compressed sensing work, and the same reason SAEs can pack 32,000+ features into 768 dimensions.

The formal guarantee: under the random walk model, if senses are sufficiently distinct (their discourse atoms don't overlap much) and the sparsity is sufficient, the recovery is provably correct up to small error terms.
The theory is beautiful, but does it actually work? The paper validates on real word embeddings:
For known polysemous words (validated against WordNet senses):
'bank': Recovered financial and river senses as distinct vectors
'rock': Recovered music genre and geological senses
'bat': Recovered animal and sports equipment senses
'spring': Recovered season, water source, and mechanical device senses

The recovered sense vectors consistently outperform the original polysemous embedding on sense-specific similarity tasks. v_bank_financial is closer to 'money' than v_bank is, and v_bank_river is closer to 'shore' than v_bank is.

The method works across multiple embedding methods — word2vec, GloVe, and others. This universality supports the theoretical claim: superposition isn't an artifact of a particular training algorithm, it's a fundamental structure of how word co-occurrence statistics encode meaning.

props.title: The Connection to SAEs and Mechanistic Interpretability

children.children: This paper, published in 2018, anticipated the core insight of the mechanistic interpretability movement by several years. The parallels are striking:

props.title: Arora 2018 vs Anthropic 2024: Same Math, Different Scale

children.children: Arora et al. 2018:
Input: 300-768 dim word embeddings
Dictionary: ~2,000 discourse atoms
Output: sparse decomposition into word senses
Claim: embeddings are superpositions of senses
Anthropic 2024:
Input: 4,096+ dim residual stream vectors
Dictionary: 34 million SAE features
Output: sparse decomposition into interpretable concepts
Claim: activations are superpositions of features
Same mathematical framework. Same core insight. Same sparse recovery technique. The difference is scale and what's being decomposed — static word vectors vs dynamic neural activations.
The intellectual lineage runs:
Olshausen & Field (1996): Sparse coding on natural images → V1-like receptive fields
Arora et al. (2018): Sparse coding on word embeddings → word senses (THIS PAPER)
Zhang/Olshausen (2019): Dictionary learning on word embeddings → interpretable word factors
Panigrahi et al. (2019): Word2Sense — sparse interpretable embeddings by design
Yun/Olshausen/LeCun (2021): Dictionary learning on transformer hidden states → transformer factors
Cunningham/Bricken et al. (2023): Sparse autoencoders on LLM activations → monosemantic features
Anthropic (2024): Scaling to 34M features on Claude 3 Sonnet
Arora et al. proved the PRINCIPLE. The rest was scaling it up.
This paper has direct implications for the Schankian operators research:
Polysemous verbs:

children: 'Shot' in 'cop shot Frank' vs 'shot a glance' — different operators! Sense decomposition could help assign the right operator by first decomposing the verb embedding into its senses.

children.children: Operator features as discourse atoms:

children: Our learned operator space might have a similar structure — operators decompose into 'operator atoms' that describe fine-grained action types.

children.children: SAE-guided diffusion:

children: Our research direction on using SAE features for diffusion is building directly on this lineage. The discourse atoms → SAE features → diffusion latent space pipeline is the same principle applied three times.

props.question: Why is the superposition structure in word embeddings useful rather than a problem?
options: It compresses information efficiently — multiple senses stored in one vector
It makes word vectors easier to train
It reduces the vocabulary size needed
It eliminates the need for contextualized embeddings
props.explanation: Superposition is an efficient compression scheme. Instead of needing separate vectors for every sense of every word (which would be huge), the model packs multiple senses into one vector. Since senses are sparse (most words have only 1-3 active senses), and high-dimensional spaces support many nearly-orthogonal directions, this compression is nearly lossless — and recoverable via sparse coding.

children.children: The paper has important limitations that motivated subsequent work:
Static embeddings only:

children: This analysis applies to word2vec/GloVe, not contextualized models like BERT. Yun et al. (2021) extended the approach to transformers.

children.children: Sense granularity:

children: The ~2,000 discourse atoms may not capture very fine-grained distinctions. Modern SAEs use millions of features for this reason.

children.children: Frequency dependence:

children: Rare senses may be overwhelmed by common ones in the superposition. If 'bank' is used 99% financially, the river sense signal is very weak.

children.children: No causal analysis:

children: The paper shows structure EXISTS but doesn't show how models USE that structure during inference. Mechanistic interpretability added this causal dimension.

children.children: Despite these limitations, the paper established a fundamental insight that grew into one of the most productive research directions in AI interpretability. The superposition hypothesis — that neural representations compress multiple concepts into shared dimensions via sparsity — is now central to understanding how LLMs work.
